places.name: Chapter One by Mickael Viljanen
places.address: 18-19 Parnell Square N, Rotunda, Dublin 1, D01 T3V8, Ireland
places.price: $$$ — two Michelin stars; tasting menu, book well ahead
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's flagship fine-dining room beneath the Dublin Writers Museum, where chef Mickael Viljanen plates refined modern Irish cooking. A special-occasion benchmark.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call the tasting menu and service world-class, though it's expensive and reservations are hard to come by.
places.localTip: The lunch menu is a relative bargain compared with dinner — book it weeks in advance.
places.story: Chapter One sits in the vaulted basement of a Georgian townhouse on Parnell Square, the literary heart of old Dublin. Finnish-born chef Mickael Viljanen took the kitchen and pushed it to two Michelin stars, weaving Irish produce into dishes of startling precision. It's the room Dubliners save up for when life calls for something unforgettable.
places.highlight: Don't miss the signature foie gras 'sandwich'.

places.name: The Winding Stair
places.address: 40 Lower Ormond Quay, Dublin 1, D01 R9Y5, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A beloved restaurant above a historic bookshop, serving seasonal Irish cooking with sweeping views of the Ha'penny Bridge and the Liffey.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners praise the views, the brown bread and the seafood, while noting tables are tight and it gets booked out.
places.localTip: Ask for a window table at dusk for the bridge view, and browse the bookshop downstairs first.
places.story: Named after a Yeats poem, The Winding Stair began as one of Dublin's most cherished bookshops in the 1970s before reopening as a restaurant overlooking the river. The kitchen champions small Irish producers and farmhouse cheeses. From the window you look straight onto the cast-iron Ha'penny Bridge, glowing as the light fades.

places.name: The Pig's Ear
places.address: 4 Nassau St, Dublin 2, D02 PD89, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A polished modern-Irish restaurant across three floors of a Georgian house, overlooking the playing fields of Trinity College.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the shepherd's pie, the value lunch and the Trinity view, with a few noting the staircases are steep.
places.localTip: Request a front table on an upper floor for the green vista across Trinity's cricket pitch.
places.story: The Pig's Ear made its name turning Irish comfort food into something refined, its slow-cooked shepherd's pie almost a Dublin institution. Tucked behind a discreet door on Nassau Street, it climbs three storeys of a classic Georgian townhouse. The top-floor windows frame Trinity's cricket grounds like a private painting.

places.name: Leo Burdock
places.address: 2 Werburgh St, Dublin 8, D08 KD57, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's most famous fish-and-chip shop, frying since 1913 just up the hill from Christ Church Cathedral.
places.googleReviewSummary: Fans call it a rite of passage with generous portions, though some say it's pricey for a chipper and there's no seating.
places.localTip: There's nowhere to sit — take your bag of chips to the cathedral grounds nearby.
places.story: Leo Burdock has been frying fish in batter since 1913, making it Dublin's oldest chipper and a pilgrimage for visitors and celebrities alike. The walls once boasted a 'hall of fame' of famous customers. Order cod and chips wrapped in paper, then eat them warm in the shadow of Christ Church.
places.highlight: Don't miss the fresh cod with a bag of chips.

places.name: The Hairy Lemon
places.address: 41-42 Stephen St Lower, Dublin 2, D02 KE03, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A characterful old pub serving traditional Dublin coddle and other Irish staples, with a film cameo to its name.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the coddle, the cosy snugs and the friendly staff, while a few find it touristy.
places.localTip: Order the Dublin coddle — sausage, bacon and potato stew you'll struggle to find elsewhere.
places.story: The Hairy Lemon earned its odd name from a local dog-catcher with a sour face, and even featured in the film The Commitments. Inside, snugs and old prints set the scene for Dublin coddle, the city's humble stew of sausage, rashers and potato. It's the kind of warm, low-ceilinged room that feels like old Dublin.

places.name: FX Buckley Steakhouse
places.address: 2 Crow St, Temple Bar, Dublin 2, D02 VF68, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A family butcher's steakhouse serving dry-aged Irish beef in the heart of Temple Bar.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the steak quality and service, though it's on the pricier side and busy at weekends.
places.localTip: The Buckleys are butchers first — trust the staff's cut recommendation for the day.
places.story: The Buckley family has been butchering in Dublin for five generations, so it makes sense their steakhouse takes beef seriously. They hang and dry-age their own Irish cuts before they ever hit the grill. Ask for the chateaubriand to share and you'll taste why locals rate it above flashier rivals.

places.name: L. Mulligan Grocer
places.address: 18 Stoneybatter, Dublin 7, D07 XW93, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A quirky Stoneybatter gastropub pairing inventive Irish food with a deep craft-beer and whiskey list, menus served in old books.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for the scotch eggs, beer pairings and characterful room, with a few noting it's small and books up.
places.localTip: It's in arty Stoneybatter, just off the tourist trail — pair the famous scotch egg with a local craft beer.
places.story: L. Mulligan Grocer turned a former grocer's shop in bohemian Stoneybatter into one of Dublin's most loved gastropubs. The menus arrive tucked inside vintage hardbacks, and the kitchen leans hard into Irish ingredients and beer pairings. Their scotch egg has a near-mythic reputation among Dublin food lovers.

places.name: Brother Hubbard
places.address: 153 Capel St, Dublin 1, D01 KF66, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A bright, much-loved all-day café on Capel Street known for Middle-Eastern-inflected brunch and excellent coffee.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners praise the brunch plates, baking and warm service, though there's often a wait at weekends.
places.localTip: The back courtyard is a sun-trap in summer — and the freshly baked goods sell out by afternoon.
places.story: Brother Hubbard grew from a small café into a Capel Street favourite by blending Irish freshness with the flavours of the Levant. Think slow-cooked eggs, dukkah and house breads, all baked on-site. It helped turn this once-overlooked street into one of Dublin's most exciting food strips.

places.name: 3fe Coffee (Grand Canal)
places.address: 32-34 Grand Canal St Lower, Dublin 2, D02 PX52, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: The flagship of Ireland's most influential specialty roaster, a temple for filter coffee and espresso near the canal.
places.googleReviewSummary: Coffee nerds adore the brews and brunch, though some find it minimal and a touch pricey.
places.localTip: Ask the barista which single-origin filter is on — they roast it themselves and love to talk it through.
places.story: 3fe — short for 'third floor espresso' — began in a hotel basement and went on to reshape how Dublin drinks coffee. Founder Colin Harmon turned a city of milky cups into one obsessed with single origins and careful extraction. This bright canal-side room is the mothership for that quiet revolution.
places.highlight: Don't miss a hand-brewed filter of the day.

places.name: Bewley's Grafton Street
places.address: 78/79 Grafton St, Dublin 2, D02 W729, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's grand historic café on Grafton Street, famous for its Harry Clarke stained glass, sticky buns and coffee since 1927.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the atmosphere and the stained glass, while some say it's touristy and prices have crept up.
places.localTip: Sit upstairs near the Harry Clarke windows, and order the legendary sticky bun with your coffee.
places.story: Bewley's has anchored Grafton Street since 1927, its Art Deco frontage and Harry Clarke stained-glass windows part of Dublin's soul. James Joyce mentioned it, and generations courted, gossiped and sheltered from the rain over its coffee. After a careful restoration, the old café aromas are back.

places.name: Two Boys Brew
places.address: 375 North Circular Rd, Phibsborough, Dublin 7, D07 XF66, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A stylish Phibsborough café that helped put the northside on Dublin's brunch map, with excellent coffee and plated brunch.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars love the brunch and flat whites, though weekend waits can be long.
places.localTip: Go midweek to skip the queue, and the corner banquette is the spot for people-watching.
places.story: Two Boys Brew brought third-wave coffee culture to Phibsborough, a workaday northside neighbourhood that didn't have much like it before. The owners' eye for design and serious coffee turned it into a destination. It's now a local hub where the flat whites are as carefully made as the brunch plates.

places.name: Clement & Pekoe
places.address: 50 William St S, Dublin 2, D02 XW63, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A cosy, plant-filled café off South William Street prized for excellent coffee, a wall of loose-leaf teas and homemade cake.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the relaxed vibe and tea selection, though seating is limited at peak times.
places.localTip: Browse the wall of tea tins — they'll brew almost any of them by the pot.
places.story: Clement & Pekoe sits on one of Dublin's most fashionable little streets, a hideaway of dark wood, hanging plants and a glowing wall of tea caddies. It's the kind of café where you can lose an afternoon over a pot of single-estate tea. Locals treat it as a sanctuary from the Grafton Street crowds a block away.

places.name: Kaph
places.address: 31 Drury St, Dublin 2, D02 XE02, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A sleek two-storey coffee bar on Drury Street, a magnet for Dublin's creative crowd and a reliable cup.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for the coffee and design-led space, with some noting it can get crowded and seats fill fast.
places.localTip: Grab a window seat upstairs to watch the Drury Street fashion parade below.
places.story: Kaph helped make Drury Street the coolest coffee corner in the city, all pared-back concrete and good music. It draws designers, stylists and students who linger over flat whites and laptops. On a sunny day the crowd spills onto the street, turning the block into an impromptu social scene.

places.name: Queen of Tarts (Cow's Lane)
places.address: 3-4 Cow's Lane, Dame St, Temple Bar, Dublin 2, D02 ET99, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A charming home-baking café famous for fruit tarts, scones and proper breakfasts near Dublin Castle.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors adore the cakes and cosy feel, though it's small and often has a queue.
places.localTip: The Cow's Lane branch is bigger and brighter than the original — go early for a window seat.
places.story: Queen of Tarts began as a tiny shopfront run by two sisters who learned to bake in New York before bringing it home to Dublin. Their flaky tarts and towering cakes quickly built a devoted following. Tucked beside cobbled Cow's Lane, it's a sweet pause between Dublin Castle and Temple Bar.
places.highlight: Don't miss a slice of the raspberry and almond tart.

places.name: Trinity College & The Book of Kells
places.address: College Green, Dublin 2, D02 PN40, Ireland
places.price: $$ — timed ticket for Book of Kells
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's oldest university, home to the ninth-century Book of Kells and the breathtaking barrel-vaulted Long Room library.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are awed by the Long Room and manuscript, while many warn to book a timed slot to avoid long queues.
places.localTip: Book the earliest morning slot online — the Long Room is far calmer before the coach tours arrive.
places.story: Founded in 1592, Trinity College is a 47-acre island of cobbles and Georgian calm in the city centre. Its Old Library guards the Book of Kells, a dazzling illuminated gospel made by monks around 800 AD. Upstairs, the 65-metre Long Room — lined with marble busts and 200,000 ancient books — is one of the most beautiful rooms on earth.
places.highlight: Don't miss standing under the Long Room's great oak arches.

places.name: Guinness Storehouse
places.address: St James's Gate, Ushers, Dublin 8, D08 VF8H, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's most-visited attraction, a seven-storey pint-glass-shaped museum of Guinness ending with a 360° rooftop bar.
places.googleReviewSummary: Most love the Gravity Bar views and the history, though some find it crowded and commercial.
places.localTip: Book online in advance and head straight up to the Gravity Bar first thing for the city panorama.
places.story: At the heart of the St James's Gate brewery, where Arthur Guinness signed a famously cheeky 9,000-year lease in 1759, the Storehouse tells the story of the black stuff. The building is shaped like a giant pint glass rising through seven floors. At the top, the Gravity Bar serves your complimentary Guinness above a 360-degree sweep of Dublin's rooftops.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Gravity Bar at the top.

places.name: Dublin Castle
places.address: Dame St, Dublin 2, D02 DX88, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A 13th-century castle turned seat of power, with opulent State Apartments, a medieval undercroft and the Chester Beatty library beside it.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the State Apartments and history, though some say the exterior is less castle-like than expected.
places.localTip: The courtyard and the free Chester Beatty Library next door are wonderful even if you skip the paid tour.
places.story: For 700 years Dublin Castle was the centre of British rule in Ireland, until power was handed over here in 1922. Beneath the grand State Apartments lie Viking and medieval foundations you can still walk through. It's where Irish presidents are now inaugurated — a place where the whole story of the city is layered underfoot.

places.name: St Patrick's Cathedral
places.address: St Patrick's Close, Wood Quay, Dublin 8, D08 H6X3, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's largest cathedral, founded in 1191, where satirist Jonathan Swift served as dean and is buried.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers admire the architecture and Swift connection, though some note the admission fee for a church.
places.localTip: The adjoining St Patrick's Park is a lovely free spot for a rest with the spire behind you.
places.story: Built beside the well where St Patrick is said to have baptised converts, this medieval cathedral has stood since 1191. Jonathan Swift, author of Gulliver's Travels, was its dean for over 30 years and lies buried in the nave with his own scathing epitaph overhead. The cool stone interior hums with eight centuries of Dublin history.

places.name: Christ Church Cathedral
places.address: Christchurch Pl, Wood Quay, Dublin 8, D08 TF98, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's oldest cathedral, founded around 1030, with a vast medieval crypt and the curious mummified 'cat and rat'.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the atmospheric crypt and architecture, with some noting the entry fee and steep streets nearby.
places.localTip: Don't skip the crypt — it's one of the oldest structures in Dublin and home to 'Tom and Jerry', a mummified cat and rat.
places.story: Christ Church was founded by Dublin's Viking king around 1030 and rebuilt in stone by the Normans. Its crypt is one of the largest in Britain or Ireland, and shelters two strange relics: a cat and a rat found mummified inside an organ pipe, now nicknamed Tom and Jerry. A covered bridge links it to the medieval museum next door.

places.name: Kilmainham Gaol
places.address: Inchicore Rd, Kilmainham, Dublin 8, D08 RK28, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A former prison central to Ireland's fight for independence, where leaders of the 1916 Rising were executed.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call the tour deeply moving and the guides excellent, but stress tickets sell out days ahead.
places.localTip: Book online a week or more in advance — same-day tickets are almost never available.
places.story: Kilmainham Gaol held — and executed — many of the heroes of Ireland's struggle for freedom, including the leaders of the 1916 Easter Rising. The haunting East Wing, with its skylit galleries, has appeared in countless films. Standing in the stonebreakers' yard where the rebels were shot is one of the most powerful experiences in Dublin.
places.highlight: Don't miss the soaring East Wing gallery.

places.name: St Stephen's Green
places.address: St Stephen's Green, Dublin 2, D02 X285, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's beloved Victorian city-centre park, with ponds, fountains and flower beds at the top of Grafton Street.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the calm, the ducks and the bandstand, though benches fill fast on sunny lunchtimes.
places.localTip: Enter through the Fusiliers' Arch at the Grafton Street corner and follow the pond loop.
places.story: Once a common where public hangings took place, St Stephen's Green was landscaped into a genteel Victorian park and opened to the public by Lord Ardilaun of the Guinness family. Bullet marks from the 1916 Rising still scar its arch. Today office workers, students and ducks share its lawns at the green lungs of the city.

places.name: Ha'penny Bridge
places.address: Bachelors Walk, North City, Dublin 1, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's graceful cast-iron pedestrian bridge over the Liffey, an icon of the city since 1816.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find it pretty and photogenic, especially lit at night, though it's busy and not large.
places.localTip: Shoot it from the boardwalk on the north quays at dusk when the lamps switch on.
places.story: When the Ha'penny Bridge opened in 1816, crossing the river by ferry cost a halfpenny — so the new bridge charged the same toll, and the name stuck. For over a century it was the only pedestrian bridge across the Liffey. Its white-painted arch, mirrored in the dark water at night, is one of Dublin's most photographed sights.

places.name: Stoneybatter
places.address: Manor St, Stoneybatter, Dublin 7, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A characterful northside village of red-brick cottages, indie cafés and craft pubs, often called Dublin's coolest neighbourhood.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals praise the village feel and food scene, while some note parking is tight on the narrow streets.
places.localTip: Wander the 'Cowtown' cottage streets, then refuel at a craft pub like Walsh's on Stoneybatter.
places.story: Stoneybatter follows the line of an ancient road the Vikings once walked into Dublin, and its tiny artisan-cottage streets still feel like a village. Once a working-class quarter known as Cowtown for its cattle markets, it's been reborn with coffee roasters, vintage shops and craft pubs. Locals fiercely protect its low-rise, neighbourly charm.

places.name: George's Street Arcade
places.address: S Great George's St, Dublin 2, D02 PK24, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A red-brick Victorian covered market from 1881, packed with vintage clothing, records, jewellery and food stalls.
places.googleReviewSummary: Shoppers love the indie stalls and atmosphere, though some say it's small and a few units sit empty.
places.localTip: Hunt for vintage finds and second-hand records, then grab a crepe from one of the food stalls.
places.story: George's Street Arcade opened in 1881 as one of Europe's first purpose-built shopping arcades, its Gothic red-brick façade unmistakable. Generations of Dubliners have rummaged its stalls for vinyl, vintage coats and curios. It's a wonderfully unpolished antidote to the chain stores of nearby Grafton Street.

places.name: Howth
places.address: Howth Harbour, Howth, Co. Dublin, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A fishing village on a peninsula at the end of the DART line, with a working harbour, seafood and clifftop walks.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the cliff walk and seafood, though the trails can be windy and busy on weekends.
places.localTip: Take the DART out, walk the cliff loop, then eat seafood chowder by the harbour before heading back.
places.story: Howth sits at the tip of a rugged peninsula a half-hour DART ride from the centre, where Dublin meets the sea. Trawlers still land their catch in the harbour, and seals patrol the pier hoping for scraps. The clifftop loop, with its heather and lighthouse views across to Ireland's Eye, is the city's favourite escape.
places.highlight: Don't miss the cliff walk to the Baily Lighthouse view.

places.name: Sandycove & Forty Foot
places.address: Sandycove Point, Sandycove, Co. Dublin, A96 E0F2, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A genteel seaside village with the Forty Foot bathing place and the James Joyce Tower where Ulysses opens.
places.googleReviewSummary: Swimmers love the bracing dip and views, though the rocks are slippery and the water is cold year-round.
places.localTip: Brave a quick dip at the Forty Foot like the locals — there are steps and it's swum all year.
places.story: The Forty Foot has been a swimming spot for over 250 years, once men-only and now open to all the hardy souls who plunge in at dawn. Above it stands the Martello tower where James Joyce briefly lived — and where the opening scene of Ulysses unfolds. On Christmas morning, crowds gather to leap into the freezing Irish Sea.

places.name: The Liberty Market
places.address: 71 Meath St, The Liberties, Dublin 8, D08 KF96, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A no-frills indoor market in the old Liberties, full of bric-a-brac, fabrics and proper old-Dublin character.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals enjoy the bargains and banter, though some find it dated and not aimed at tourists.
places.localTip: It runs Thursday to Saturday — come for the chat and old-Dublin atmosphere as much as the stalls.
places.story: The Liberty Market sits in the heart of the Liberties, the medieval weavers' quarter that once lay outside the city walls and made its own rules. Stalls heaped with fabric, clothes and curios carry on a trading tradition centuries old. It's gloriously unpolished — the sound of broad Dublin accents bartering is half the fun.

places.name: Grand Canal at Portobello
places.address: Grand Canal, Portobello Harbour, Dublin 8, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A leafy stretch of canal lined with locks, swans and pubs, a favourite local spot for a riverside pint in summer.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals love the relaxed canal-side drinks and walks, though it gets very busy on warm evenings.
places.localTip: On a sunny evening, grab a takeaway pint and sit on the lock gates with the Portobello crowd.
places.story: The Grand Canal once carried barges from Dublin to the Shannon, and the stretch through Portobello is its prettiest. Poet Patrick Kavanagh adored it so much he asked to be remembered here — and a bench by the water bears his bronze likeness. On the first warm evening of the year, half of young Dublin gathers on its banks.

places.name: Dublin Literary Pub Crawl
places.address: The Duke, 9 Duke St, Dublin 2, D02 KX60, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A theatrical walking tour led by actors who perform Joyce, Beckett and Behan between historic literary pubs.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests find the actors hilarious and informative, though it involves a fair bit of walking and standing.
places.localTip: It starts upstairs at The Duke — arrive early to grab a pint before the actors begin.
places.story: Dublin is a UNESCO City of Literature, home to four Nobel laureates, and this crawl celebrates that legacy with two actors performing scenes between famous pubs. You'll hear Joyce, Beckett, Wilde and Behan brought to life over a pint. It's history, theatre and a pub session rolled into one gloriously Dublin evening.

places.name: Jameson Distillery Bow St.
places.address: Bow St, Smithfield Village, Dublin 7, D07 N9VH, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: The original 1780 Jameson distillery, now a slick visitor experience with guided tastings and cocktail classes.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the comparative tasting and guides, though some find it more marketing than working distillery.
places.localTip: Upgrade to the whiskey-blending or cocktail experience if you want more than the standard tour.
places.story: John Jameson set up his distillery on Bow Street in 1780, and for two centuries the smell of malt drifted over Smithfield. Production has moved to Cork, but the original site now guides visitors through the story of Irish whiskey with a comparative tasting against Scotch and bourbon. Few leave without a new favourite dram.

places.name: GAA Museum & Croke Park Tour
places.address: Croke Park, Jones' Rd, Drumcondra, Dublin 3, D03 P6K7, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A museum and stadium tour explaining Ireland's national sports, hurling and Gaelic football, with a rooftop Skyline walk.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love learning the sports and the Skyline views, though the rooftop walk costs extra.
places.localTip: Add the Skyline rooftop tour for panoramic views over the whole north city.
places.story: Croke Park is the spiritual home of Gaelic games, an 82,000-seat cathedral of hurling and football. Its terraces witnessed Bloody Sunday in 1920, when British forces opened fire on a crowd during a match. The museum lets you try your hand at the sports, while the Skyline walk takes you out onto the stadium roof.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Skyline rooftop walk.

places.name: Viking Splash Tours
places.address: St Stephen's Green N, Dublin 2, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A fun amphibious-vehicle tour that drives Dublin's streets then splashes into the Grand Canal Dock, with a Viking theme.
places.googleReviewSummary: Families love the humour and the splash into the water, though some adults find it cheesy.
places.localTip: Bring a light rain jacket and wear the Viking helmet — the roars at passers-by are half the fun.
places.story: Viking Splash Tours run in reconditioned World War II amphibious 'Ducks', painted up like longships. Your guide leads the whole bus in Viking roars at startled pedestrians before plunging into the water at the docklands. It's silly, joyful and a surprisingly good crash course in Dublin's Norse origins.

places.name: Howth Cliff Walk & Seafari
places.address: West Pier, Howth Harbour, Howth, Co. Dublin, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A boat trip from Howth harbour out to the seabird colonies and seals of Ireland's Eye island.
places.googleReviewSummary: Passengers love spotting seals and puffins, though trips depend on weather and the sea can be choppy.
places.localTip: Combine the boat trip with the cliff loop for the best of Howth in a single half-day.
places.story: Just off Howth lies Ireland's Eye, an uninhabited island ringed by sea-stacks where puffins, gannets and seals make their home. Small boats putter out from the harbour to circle it in summer. Pair it with the heather-covered cliff path and you've had one of the most beautiful days the Dublin coast can offer.

places.name: EPIC The Irish Emigration Museum
places.address: The CHQ Building, Custom House Quay, Dublin 1, D01 Y6H7, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: An award-winning interactive museum tracing how 10 million Irish emigrants shaped the world, in a vaulted dockside cellar.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it engaging and high-tech, though some say it's text-heavy and best with time to spare.
places.localTip: Pick up a 'passport' at the start and get it stamped in each gallery — great for kids and adults alike.
places.story: Voted Europe's leading tourist attraction more than once, EPIC fills the brick-vaulted cellars of a former tobacco and wine store on the docks. Through touchscreens and projections it tells how the Irish — from rebels to writers to musicians — carried their culture across the globe. It's a moving, surprisingly playful tribute to the diaspora.

places.name: National Gallery of Ireland
places.address: Merrion Square W, Dublin 2, D02 K303, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's national art collection, free to enter, with Caravaggio, Vermeer and the country's finest Jack B. Yeats holdings.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the free entry and the Yeats and Caravaggio rooms, though the layout can be confusing.
places.localTip: Seek out the Caravaggio 'Taking of Christ' — lost for centuries and rediscovered in a Dublin Jesuit house.
places.story: The National Gallery on Merrion Square holds Ireland's greatest art, from Italian masters to a room devoted to Jack B. Yeats, brother of the poet. Its star is Caravaggio's Taking of Christ, lost for two centuries before being recognised on the wall of a Jesuit dining room in Dublin. Best of all, admission is free.
places.highlight: Don't miss Caravaggio's 'The Taking of Christ'.

places.name: Irish Museum of Modern Art (IMMA)
places.address: Royal Hospital, Military Rd, Kilmainham, Dublin 8, D08 FW31, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's leading modern and contemporary art museum, set in the magnificent 17th-century Royal Hospital Kilmainham.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors praise the building, gardens and rotating shows, though some find the collection on display limited.
places.localTip: The formal gardens and courtyard are free and gorgeous even if you skip the galleries.
places.story: IMMA occupies the Royal Hospital Kilmainham, a stately 1684 home for old soldiers modelled on Les Invalides in Paris. Its colonnaded courtyard and walled gardens are a destination in themselves. Inside, bold contemporary exhibitions play against the grand classical bones of the building.

places.name: Chester Beatty Library
places.address: Dublin Castle, Dame St, Dublin 2, D02 AD92, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A free, world-class collection of manuscripts, sacred texts and Islamic and East Asian art, hidden in Dublin Castle's grounds.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are wowed by the treasures and rooftop garden, though it's easy to miss tucked behind the castle.
places.localTip: Climb to the free rooftop garden for one of the best quiet views in the city centre.
places.story: American mining magnate Alfred Chester Beatty spent a lifetime amassing one of the finest private collections of manuscripts and Eastern art on earth, then gifted it to Ireland. Tucked behind Dublin Castle, it holds ancient Qur'ans, Buddhist scrolls and early biblical papyri. It's repeatedly voted Europe's best museum — and entry is completely free.

places.name: Abbey Theatre
places.address: 26-27 Lower Abbey St, Dublin 1, D01 K0F1, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's national theatre, founded by W.B. Yeats and Lady Gregory in 1904, still staging Irish drama old and new.
places.googleReviewSummary: Theatregoers praise the productions and history, though some find the 1960s building plain.
places.localTip: Check for cheaper preview-night tickets and pre-show talks on the season's new Irish plays.
places.story: The Abbey was founded in 1904 by poet W.B. Yeats and Lady Gregory as the world's first state-subsidised theatre, championing a distinctly Irish voice on stage. Its premieres of Synge and O'Casey once sparked actual riots in the streets. It remains the beating heart of Irish theatre, nurturing the country's playwrights and actors.

places.name: National Museum of Ireland – Archaeology
places.address: Kildare St, Dublin 2, D02 FH48, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A free national museum housing Ireland's Celtic gold, Viking artefacts and eerily preserved Iron Age bog bodies.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are fascinated by the bog bodies and gold hoards, though some wish for more interpretation.
places.localTip: Head straight for the bog bodies and the Treasury with the Ardagh Chalice and Tara Brooch.
places.story: This grand Victorian museum guards the treasures of ancient Ireland — Bronze Age gold collars, the intricate Tara Brooch, and the chillingly preserved bodies of Iron Age people pulled from the bogs. Each artefact is a window into a Celtic world of kings, sacrifice and astonishing craft. And, like the other national museums, it's free.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Iron Age bog bodies.

places.name: Gallery of Photography Ireland
places.address: Meeting House Square, Temple Bar, Dublin 2, D02 X406, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's dedicated photography gallery, with changing exhibitions of Irish and international work on Temple Bar's Meeting House Square.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the curated shows and the bookshop, though the space is small and shows rotate often.
places.localTip: Pair it with the open-air Saturday food market on the same square.
places.story: On cobbled Meeting House Square in the cultural quarter of Temple Bar, this gallery has championed photography as an art form in Ireland for decades. Its bright, modern space hosts everything from documentary work to experimental images. The downstairs bookshop is a treasure trove for anyone serious about photography.

places.name: National Concert Hall
places.address: Earlsfort Terrace, Dublin 2, D02 N527, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's premier concert venue and home of the National Symphony Orchestra, in a grand former exhibition hall.
places.googleReviewSummary: Audiences praise the acoustics and programme, though some note the seating layout varies by section.
places.localTip: Look out for the free or low-cost lunchtime recitals during term time.
places.story: The National Concert Hall fills part of a building that began life as the Great Exhibition Palace of 1865 and later housed University College Dublin. Now it's the home of the National Symphony Orchestra and the country's grand stage for classical music, jazz and trad. Generations of Dubliners have had their first orchestral experience under its ceiling.

places.name: The Cobblestone
places.address: 77 King St N, Smithfield, Dublin 7, D07 TP22, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A revered Smithfield pub famed for the best traditional Irish music sessions in the city, run by a musical family.
places.googleReviewSummary: Music lovers call the sessions the real deal, though it's small and gets packed early.
places.localTip: The front bar sessions are for listening — keep the chat low while the musicians play.
places.story: The Cobblestone calls itself 'a drinking pub with a music problem', and it means it. Run by the Mulligan family of musicians, its front bar hosts nightly trad sessions that draw players from across Ireland. When the city threatened it with redevelopment, Dubliners rallied to save it — proof of how much this room matters.
places.highlight: Don't miss a nightly session in the front bar.

places.name: Whelan's
places.address: 25 Wexford St, Dublin 2, D02 H527, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's most storied live-music venue, where countless Irish and international acts played before they were famous.
places.googleReviewSummary: Gig-goers love the intimate room and history, though the floor gets hot and packed for big shows.
places.localTip: The main room balcony gives a great view if the floor is rammed — and the front bar has its own gigs.
places.story: Whelan's on Wexford Street is hallowed ground for Irish music — Jeff Buckley, Ed Sheeran and Hozier all played its little stage on the way up. The venue even featured in the film P.S. I Love You. On any given night you might catch the next big thing in a room that holds just a few hundred.

places.name: O'Donoghue's
places.address: 15 Merrion Row, Dublin 2, D02 DE61, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: The legendary pub where The Dubliners formed, still hosting nightly trad sessions just off St Stephen's Green.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the live trad and the history, though it's small and very busy with tourists.
places.localTip: Squeeze into the back room or the snug where the musicians gather in a circle.
places.story: O'Donoghue's is where the folk group The Dubliners came together in the 1960s, launching a ballad revival that swept the world. Its nicotine-warm walls are crammed with photos of the musicians who've played here. Most nights a session still strikes up in the corner, exactly as it has for decades.

places.name: Vicar Street
places.address: 58-59 Thomas St, Usher's Quay, Dublin 8, D08 X3T2, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A beloved mid-size venue in the Liberties known for superb sound, hosting music, comedy and spoken word.
places.googleReviewSummary: Audiences rate the acoustics and intimacy highly, though seated-vs-standing layout depends on the show.
places.localTip: For seated shows, the table tickets near the front are worth the splurge.
places.story: Vicar Street opened in 1998 and quickly became Dublin's favourite room for an up-close show, prized by performers for its warmth and sound. Comedians, songwriters and legends from Christy Moore to international stars return again and again. It seats around a thousand, small enough that every gig feels personal.

places.name: 3Arena
places.address: N Wall Quay, North Dock, Dublin 1, D01 F8C5, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's main indoor arena on the docklands, the stop for the world's biggest touring acts.
places.googleReviewSummary: Concertgoers praise the sound and sightlines, though exiting with the crowd onto the Luas can be slow.
places.localTip: Take the red Luas tram right to the door and avoid the post-gig parking crush entirely.
places.story: Out on the regenerated docklands, the 3Arena fills a former rail depot with up to 13,000 fans for the biggest names in music. The red Luas tram glides right to its entrance. Step outside after a show and the lights of the Samuel Beckett Bridge shimmer across the Liffey.

places.name: The Temple Bar Pub
places.address: 47-48 Temple Bar, Dublin 2, D02 N725, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: The famous red-fronted pub at the centre of Temple Bar, all flower baskets, live trad and tourist energy.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the buzz, music and photo-op, though everyone warns the pints are very expensive.
places.localTip: Worth a photo and one drink for the atmosphere — locals drink cheaper a few streets away.
places.story: With its scarlet façade dripping in flower baskets, The Temple Bar is the most photographed pub in Ireland. Inside, live music runs from afternoon to late and the whiskey selection is genuinely vast. Yes, it's touristy and pricey — but as a slice of postcard Dublin, it's hard to resist.

places.name: The Long Hall
places.address: 51 S Great George's St, Dublin 2, D02 FK21, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: One of Dublin's most beautiful Victorian pubs, all carved mahogany, chandeliers and antique clocks.
places.googleReviewSummary: Drinkers adore the gorgeous interior and well-poured pints, though it gets crowded and seating is scarce.
places.localTip: Order a Guinness and let it settle properly — and look up at the ornate old clock and mirrors.
places.story: The Long Hall has poured pints since the 19th century behind one of the most ornate Victorian bar interiors in Europe — crimson, carved wood and crystal chandeliers. Bruce Springsteen famously called in for a pint. It's a place to slow down and admire a pub frozen in gilded time.
places.highlight: Don't miss the perfectly poured Guinness.

places.name: The Palace Bar
places.address: 21 Fleet St, Temple Bar, Dublin 2, D02 H950, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A wonderfully preserved Victorian pub long beloved of journalists and writers, with a fine Irish whiskey list.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars praise the authentic feel and whiskey selection, though the back snug fills up fast.
places.localTip: Head to the skylit back snug for the prettiest spot, and try a pour from their own-label whiskey.
places.story: The Palace Bar was the unofficial newsroom of Irish journalism and the favourite haunt of writers like Patrick Kavanagh and Flann O'Brien. Little has changed: mahogany, mirrors and a stained-glass skylight over the back room. It even bottles its own Irish whiskey, a proper Dublin institution.

places.name: The George
places.address: 89 S Great George's St, Dublin 2, D02 ED70, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's oldest and most famous gay bar and nightclub, a riotous institution with drag, bingo and dancing.
places.googleReviewSummary: Crowds love the inclusive vibe and Shirley's bingo, though it gets very busy and queues form late.
places.localTip: Sunday-night drag bingo with Shirley Temple Bar is a Dublin rite of passage — get there early.
places.story: The George has been the heart of Dublin's LGBTQ+ nightlife since 1985, its purple frontage a beacon on George's Street. From legendary Sunday-night drag bingo to thumping late-night dancefloors, it welcomes absolutely everyone. It's joyful, irreverent and an essential part of the city after dark.

places.name: The Workman's Club
places.address: 10 Wellington Quay, Temple Bar, Dublin 2, D02 VX36, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A multi-room club and live venue on the quays, with indie DJs, gigs and a rooftop terrace.
places.googleReviewSummary: Crowds love the variety of rooms and music, though queues and the smoking area can get jammed.
places.localTip: Explore upstairs — there are several rooms and a rooftop you might otherwise miss.
places.story: Set in a former working men's club on the Liffey quays, The Workman's keeps the old-school name and adds DJs, live bands and dancing across multiple floors. Indie kids, students and night owls fill its warren of rooms until late. The rooftop terrace is a rare open-air spot to catch your breath mid-night-out.

places.name: The Blind Pig Speakeasy
places.address: Powerscourt Townhouse, S William St, Dublin 2, D02 H470, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A hidden 1920s-style speakeasy serving theatrical craft cocktails behind an unmarked door near Powerscourt.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the secret-bar theatre and inventive drinks, though it requires booking and is on the pricey side.
places.localTip: Reservation only — you'll be given a password and an address when you book.
places.story: The Blind Pig leans into Prohibition romance: book ahead, get a secret password, and you're led to a glamorous hidden bar of velvet and low light. The cocktails arrive with flourish and flair, sometimes a little theatre on the side. It's one of Dublin's most fun nights for anyone who loves a proper cocktail.

places.name: The Porterhouse Temple Bar
places.address: 16-18 Parliament St, Temple Bar, Dublin 2, D02 KW80, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Ireland's original craft brewpub spread over multiple levels, with its own stouts, ales and nightly live music.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beer fans love the house brews and tasting trays, though it can be loud and busy at night.
places.localTip: Order the sampler tray to taste their stouts side by side — the Oyster Stout is a talking point.
places.story: The Porterhouse opened in 1996 as Dublin's first craft brewpub, brave enough to take on the city's Guinness monoculture with its own range of stouts and ales. Spread across warren-like levels, it pairs house beer with live music most nights. It proved Dubliners would happily drink something other than the black stuff.

places.name: Sophie's at The Dean
places.address: 33 Harcourt St, Saint Kevin's, Dublin 2, D02 NY55, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A glass-walled rooftop bar and restaurant atop The Dean hotel, with some of the best skyline views in the city.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the views and buzzy vibe, though it's pricey and books out for weekend evenings.
places.localTip: Reserve a table by the windows at sunset for the best view over the Dublin Mountains.
places.story: Perched on the roof of the hip Dean hotel, Sophie's wraps a buzzy bar and pizza restaurant in floor-to-ceiling glass. The view sweeps from the city's church spires out to the Dublin Mountains. It's where Dubliners come to feel a little glamorous as the sun goes down over the rooftops.
places.highlight: Don't miss sunset over the Dublin Mountains.

places.name: Roe & Co Distillery
places.address: 92 James's St, Dublin 8, D08 ET27, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A sleek working distillery in a former Guinness power station, offering whiskey tours and hands-on cocktail and blending classes.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the design, guides and interactive flavour sessions, though it's smaller than the big brand tours.
places.localTip: Book the cocktail-making class rather than just the tour for a more hands-on hour.
places.story: Roe & Co revived a name that once made Dublin one of the whiskey capitals of the world, built inside a converted Guinness power station in the Liberties. The experience leans interactive, letting you build cocktails or blend your own whiskey at the bar. It's a stylish nod to Thomas Street's lost distilling heritage.

places.name: Cooks Academy
places.address: 19 South William St, Dublin 2, D02 NX30, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A city-centre cookery school running hands-on evening and day classes, from Irish dishes to pasta and bread.
places.googleReviewSummary: Students praise the friendly chefs and practical skills, though spots in popular classes fill fast.
places.localTip: The evening 'cook and dine' classes end with everyone eating what they've made — great for date night.
places.story: Cooks Academy sits on fashionable South William Street, teaching everyone from nervous beginners to keen amateurs in its bright kitchens. Classes range from Irish brown bread to fresh pasta and curries. The best ones finish with the whole group sitting down to eat the meal they've just cooked together.

places.name: The Chocolate Factory / Designist Workshops
places.address: 26 King's Inns St, Dublin 1, D01 EOC8, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A creative hub in a former chocolate factory hosting pottery, jewellery and craft workshops with local makers.
places.googleReviewSummary: Participants enjoy the hands-on classes and creative space, though the building can be tricky to find.
places.localTip: Check the schedule ahead — workshops are run by independent makers and dates vary.
places.story: Once a working chocolate factory, this northside building is now a warren of artist studios and maker spaces. Independent ceramicists and craftspeople run drop-in and evening workshops here, from throwing pots to making jewellery. It's a window into Dublin's thriving, slightly hidden creative scene.

places.name: The Pepper Pot Baking (Cake Café)
places.address: Pleasants Pl, Saint Kevin's, Dublin 8, D08 V045, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A charming hidden café behind a craft shop, known for homemade bakes and occasional baking and craft workshops.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the secret-garden feel and cakes, though the courtyard is small and weather-dependent.
places.localTip: It's tucked behind a stationery shop — follow the signs through to the hidden courtyard.
places.story: Hidden behind a craft and stationery shop in leafy Portobello, this little café opens onto a secret courtyard garden. It built its name on homemade cakes, soda bread and seasonal bakes using Irish ingredients. On occasion it runs small baking and craft sessions — a sweet, low-key corner of creative Dublin.

places.name: Merrion Square Georgian Doors
places.address: Merrion Square, Dublin 2, D02 NP23, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A perfect Georgian square ringed by the candy-coloured front doors that became a symbol of Dublin.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the colourful doors and the Wilde statue, though the park gate hours vary.
places.localTip: Shoot the doors on the north and west sides in morning light, then visit the reclining Oscar Wilde statue in the park.
places.story: Merrion Square is Georgian Dublin at its finest, its red-brick terraces fronted by the brightly painted doors that fill every postcard. Legend says residents painted them different colours to tell their identical houses apart. In the leafy central park, a flamboyant statue of Oscar Wilde reclines on a rock, watching it all with a smirk.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Oscar Wilde statue in the park.

places.name: Samuel Beckett Bridge
places.address: Samuel Beckett Bridge, Dublin 1, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Santiago Calatrava's harp-shaped cable-stayed bridge, the gleaming icon of Dublin's modern docklands.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photographers love the harp shape and reflections, though it's most striking at night and at blue hour.
places.localTip: Shoot it at blue hour from the south quay for the harp lit up against the dusk sky.
places.story: Designed by Spanish architect Santiago Calatrava, the Samuel Beckett Bridge is shaped like a harp lying on its side — a nod to Ireland's national emblem. It can even rotate to let ships pass. Lit up at night above the black water, it's become the signature image of the regenerated docklands.

places.name: Grafton Street
places.address: Grafton St, Dublin 2, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: Dublin's bustling pedestrian shopping street, alive with buskers, flower sellers and red-brick storefronts.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the street performers and energy, though it's crowded and the shops are chain-heavy.
places.localTip: Come early morning for clean shots before the crowds, or evening for the buskers in full flow.
places.story: Grafton Street has been Dublin's premier promenade for two centuries, immortalised in song and lined with red-brick shopfronts. Buskers have launched careers on its cobbles — Glen Hansard and Damien Rice both played here before fame. The flower sellers, the molly-malone bustle and the live music make it the city's stage.

places.name: Phoenix Park & Papal Cross
places.address: Phoenix Park, Dublin 8, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: One of Europe's largest enclosed city parks, with wild fallow deer, the Papal Cross, and the President's residence.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love spotting the deer and the vast open space, though it's huge and best explored by bike.
places.localTip: The deer herd often grazes near the Papal Cross at dawn and dusk — keep a respectful distance.
places.story: Phoenix Park is more than twice the size of New York's Central Park, a vast green expanse where a herd of wild fallow deer has roamed since the 1660s. Within it stand the Irish President's house, Dublin Zoo, and a towering cross built for Pope John Paul II's 1979 mass before over a million people. At dawn the deer drift through the mist like something from a fairy tale.
places.highlight: Don't miss the wild deer herd.

places.name: Drury Street Umbrellas
places.address: Drury St, Dublin 2, D02 P993, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A hip city-centre street famous for its canopy of brightly coloured umbrellas strung overhead.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the umbrella photo-op and café scene, though the installation isn't always up.
places.localTip: Stand in the middle of the street and shoot straight up — best when the cafés' lights come on at dusk.
places.story: Drury Street became one of Dublin's most Instagrammed spots thanks to the rainbow of umbrellas suspended above its cobbles, a cheerful answer to Irish drizzle. The lane is also the city's coolest café and cocktail strip. Look up for the colour, look down for the well-dressed crowd spilling from the bars.

places.name: Great South Wall & Poolbeg Lighthouse
places.address: Great South Wall, Pigeon House Rd, Ringsend, Dublin 4, Ireland
places.reviewSummary: A long sea wall you can walk far out into Dublin Bay, ending at the candy-red Poolbeg Lighthouse.
places.googleReviewSummary: Walkers love the bay views and the lighthouse, though it's exposed and windy and a long there-and-back.
places.localTip: Time the walk for sunset with the Dublin Mountains behind you — but bring a windproof layer.
places.story: The Great South Wall was once the longest sea wall in the world, reaching over a kilometre out into Dublin Bay past the iconic red-and-white Poolbeg Lighthouse. Walking it feels like stepping out to sea on foot, the city shrinking behind you. The twin Poolbeg chimneys and the mountains make for one of Dublin's most dramatic skies.
places.highlight: Don't miss the walk out to the red Poolbeg Lighthouse.
